points: A map is a flat representation of an area and relies on three essential components: scale (distance), direction (cardinal and intermediate points), and symbols.
While maps are convenient, a globe is the most accurate model of the Earth as it preserves the planet's spherical shape without the distortions of flat paper.
Conventional symbols, such as those fixed by the Survey of India, ensure that maps are universally understood across different regions and languages.

summary.category: The Grid System: Latitudes and Longitudes
points: Latitudes are parallel circles running east-west, with the Equator (0°) being the largest; they also define the Earth's heat zones (Torrid, Temperate, and Frigid).
Longitudes (meridians) are semi-circles running from pole to pole, with the Prime Meridian passing through Greenwich serving as the global reference (0°).
The intersection of latitudes and longitudes forms a global grid that allows any location on Earth, such as Delhi at 29°N and 77°E, to be pinpointed precisely.
Earth's rotation (15° per hour) links longitude directly to time; places east of the Prime Meridian are ahead in time, while those to the west are behind.
To avoid local confusion, countries adopt a Standard Time; India follows Indian Standard Time (IST) based on 82.5° E, which is 5 hours 30 mins ahead of GMT.
The International Date Line, located approximately at 180° longitude, marks the point where the date changes by one day when crossed.
